question: How do I join the office hours or live workshop if the Zoom link isn’t shared with students?
answer_llm:
Students don’t join office hours or live workshops through the Zoom link directly, since that link is only shared with instructors/presenters/TAs.

Instead, you can:
- watch the session live on the DataTalksClub YouTube Channel,
- check the announcements channel on Telegram or Slack for the video URL before it starts,
- submit questions via Slido, whose link is pinned in the chat during the live session.

Don’t post questions in chat, because they may be missed.
answer_orig:
The zoom link is only published to instructors/presenters/TAs.

Students participate via YouTube Live and submit questions to Slido (link is pinned in the chat when live). The video URL should be posted in the [announcements channel on Telegram and Slack](https://t.me/dezoomcamp) before it begins. You can also watch live on the DataTalksClub [YouTube Channel](https://www.youtube.com/c/DataTalksClub).

Don’t post questions in chat as they may be missed if the room is very active.

question: How do I find my entry on the leaderboard if I can’t spot my name?
answer_llm:
Click the **“Jump to your record on the leaderboard”** link — that takes you directly to your entry.

If you want to see the name shown publicly, you can also open **“Edit Course Profile”**.
answer_orig:
When you set up your account, you are automatically assigned a random name, such as “Lucid Elbakyan.” Click on the "Jump to your record on the leaderboard" link to find your entry.

If you want to see what your Display name is, click on the "Edit Course Profile" button.

<{IMAGE:image_1}>

- **First field:** This is your nickname/displayed name. You can change it if you want to be known by your Slack username, GitHub username, or any other nickname of your choice. This is useful if you want to remain anonymous.
- **Second field:** Change this to your official name as in your identification documents—passport, national ID card, driver's license, etc. This is mandatory if you do not want "Lucid Elbakyan" on your certificate. This name will appear on your Certificate!

question: Why does the leaderboard show a random name for me instead of my real one?
answer_llm:
The leaderboard shows your separate public display name, which is assigned as a random anonymous nickname by default, not your real certificate name.

If you want to change it:
- Go to **Edit Course Profile**
- **First field:** public nickname/display name for the leaderboard
- **Second field:** official name for your certificate

So your real name stays on the certificate, while the leaderboard can remain anonymous.

question: Why does the homework content keep changing before the deadline?
answer_llm:
Because the homework isn’t final yet. If the homework title contains `[DRAFT]`, it means it’s still being worked on, so the content can change until:

- the homework form is open on the course management platform, and
- the title no longer contains `[DRAFT]`.

Until both are true, the content may still change.
answer_orig:
If the homework title contains `[DRAFT]`, it means the homework is not ready yet.

The homework is ready only when both are true:

- The homework form is open on the course management platform.
- The homework title does not contain `[DRAFT]`.

Until then, the content can still change. Working on the material or homework in advance is at your own risk, because the final version can be different.

question: Is the name I enter for the certificate visible on the public leaderboard or anywhere else online?
answer_llm: No. The name you enter for the certificate is only printed on your certificate; it is not shown on the public leaderboard or shared online. The leaderboard uses your separate display/display name instead.
answer_orig:
No. The certificate name only appears on your certificate — it isn't published online or shared with third parties. The public leaderboard uses your separate display name instead, which is a random nickname (like “Lucid Elbakyan”) and anonymous by default.

You can set both in “Edit Course Profile”: the first field is your public nickname for the leaderboard, and the second is the official name printed on your certificate. This lets you keep your real name off the public leaderboard while still having it on the certificate.

question: Why don't we use LangChain in this course when building RAG apps?
answer_llm:
We don’t use LangChain in the course because the goal is to learn the core RAG building blocks first: prompting, retrieval, indexing, and evaluation.

It’s like learning HTML, CSS, and JavaScript before using a framework like React or Angular. Once you understand the underlying pieces, frameworks become much easier to use well.
answer_orig:
LangChain is a framework for building LLM-powered apps. In this course, we first build the core pieces ourselves: prompting, retrieval, indexing, and evaluation.

Think of it like learning HTML, CSS, and JavaScript before using React or Angular. Frameworks are easier to use well once you understand what they automate.

question: Why do I get an OpenAI API error about insufficient credits when I run responses.create?
answer_llm:
Because your OpenAI API account has insufficient quota/credits. The course notes say there is **no free usage** of the OpenAI API, so `responses.create` can fail with:

- `OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits`
- or a related `429`/`insufficient_quota` error

To fix it:
1. Add credits to your OpenAI account (minimum $5).
2. Make sure you’re using a model available to your account.
3. If needed, recreate your API key and update it locally.

If you’re using OpenRouter instead of OpenAI, a similar error can happen for a different reason and may require setting a lower `max_output_tokens`.
answer_orig:

question: Why am I getting a 429 RateLimitError from OpenAI even though my code looks fine?
answer_llm:
Because in this case `429 RateLimitError` usually isn’t a “your code is wrong” problem — it means your OpenAI account has **run out of quota/credit** or you hit your account’s usage limits.

From the context, the key clues are:

- The error message says **`insufficient_quota`**
- OpenAI notes: **“You exceeded your current quota, please check your plan and billing details”**
- There is **no free usage** of the OpenAI API

What to do:

1. **Add billing credits** to your OpenAI account.
2. Make sure you’re using a **model available to your account**.
3. You may need to **create a new API key** after enabling billing.

If you’re getting this during the course with lots of notebook calls, also check for actual rate-limit pressure:
- reduce concurrency
- add retries/backoff
- cache results
- batch requests

But if the error says `insufficient_quota`, it’s a billing/quota issue, not a bug in your code.

question: Why does the course use uv instead of pip and virtualenv?
answer_llm:
The course uses **uv** because it’s **fast and convenient**. It’s an **all-in-one tool** that can replace tools like **pip** and **virtualenv** (as well as several others), so you don’t have to juggle multiple commands for Python versions, virtual environments, and dependencies.

That said, you’re **not required** to use it — **pip and virtualenv still work fine**. The lessons use `uv` mainly because it makes setup **simpler and faster** and gives **reproducible lockfiles**.
answer_orig:
The course uses uv because it's fast and convenient. It's an all-in-one tool that replaces pip, virtualenv, pip-tools, pipx, poetry, pyenv, and twine — instead of juggling several tools, you manage Python versions, virtual environments, and dependencies through one fast command with reproducible lockfiles.

You're not required to use it — pip and virtualenv still work fine — but uv makes the setup simpler and faster, which is why the lessons use it.

Official docs: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/

question: Is uv really replacing tools like pip, virtualenv, poetry, and pyenv?

It’s an **all-in-one tool** for:
- managing Python versions
- creating virtual environments
- installing dependencies
- handling lockfiles

That said, it’s **not required**. The course says **pip and virtualenv still work fine**; `uv` is just faster and simpler, which is why the lessons use it.

question: How do I keep my OpenAI or Groq API keys in a .env file and read them in Python?
answer_llm:
Store the keys in a `.env` file and load them in Python with `python-dotenv`.

1. Add `.env` to `.gitignore`:
```gitignore
.env
```

2. Create a `.env` file:
```bash
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
GROQ_API_KEY=gsk_...
```

3. Install `python-dotenv` if needed:
```bash
pip install python-dotenv
```

4. Read the keys in Python:
```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

openai_api_key = os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY")
groq_api_key = os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY")
```

Make sure the variable names in `.env` match exactly.

Yes. Add `.env` to `.gitignore` so your secret keys are never committed to GitHub.

Example:

```gitignore
.env
```

You should keep the actual keys in a local `.env` file and load them with `python-dotenv`.

question: How can I set up OPENAI_API_KEY, GROQ_API_KEY, and GEMINI_API_KEY without hardcoding them?
answer_llm:
Use a `.env` file instead of hardcoding the keys.

1. Create a `.env` file in your project root:
```bash
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
GROQ_API_KEY=gsk_...
GEMINI_API_KEY=...
```

2. Add `.env` to `.gitignore` so it is never committed:
```gitignore
.env
```

3. Load it in Python with `python-dotenv`:
```bash
pip install python-dotenv
```

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

openai_api_key = os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY")
groq_api_key = os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY")
gemini_api_key = os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY")
```

If you want the keys loaded automatically when you enter the project folder, you can also use `dirdotenv`.

question: What do I need to set up before I can call Gemini from the Module 1 notebook using the OpenAI-style endpoint?
answer_llm:
You need three things before calling Gemini from the Module 1 notebook via the OpenAI-style endpoint:

1. A Gemini API key saved in your `.env` file, e.g. `GEMINI_API_KEY`
2. An OpenAI client configured with Google’s OpenAI-compatible base URL:
   `https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/`
3. The Gemini model name in your request, such as `gemini-3.1-flash-lite`

So in practice, you load the key, create the client with `base_url`, and then pass the Gemini model name when making the call.
answer_orig:
To get started you need three things:

1. A Gemini API key saved in your `.env` file, for example as `GEMINI_API_KEY`.
2. An OpenAI client pointed at Google’s OpenAI-compatible base URL.
3. Your selected Google Gemini model name in your request.

Example code (loads the API key from `.env`, creates the Gemini client, and defines the `llm` helper):

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

def llm(instructions, user_prompt, model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite"):
    message_history = [
        {"role": "developer", "content": instructions},
        {"role": "user", "content": user_prompt}
    ]

    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=model,
        messages=message_history
    )

    return response.choices[0].message.content
```

This uses the older chat completions style via the OpenAI-compatible endpoint, whereas many course examples use the newer Responses format. That means you will need to change the notebook code in a few places, especially where it reads the model response and where it handles tools or function calls.

question: How can I create an OpenAI client that talks to Google Gemini instead of the usual OpenAI API?
answer_llm:
Use the OpenAI SDK, but point it to Google’s OpenAI-compatible Gemini endpoint and use your Gemini API key:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)
```

Then call Gemini models through `chat.completions.create`:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite",
    messages=[
        {"role": "developer", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Hello!"}
    ]
)

print(response.choices[0].message.content)
```

If you want structured output, you can also use `chat.completions.parse` with a Pydantic model instead of `responses.create`.

question: Why do the course examples need changes when I use Gemini with chat completions instead of the newer Responses API?
answer_llm:
Because Gemini via the OpenAI-compatible endpoint is still using **chat completions**, not the newer **Responses API**.

So the course examples need changes in two main places:

- **How you read the answer**
  - Responses API: `response.output`
  - Chat completions: `response.choices[0].message.content`

- **How tools / structured output are handled**
  - The Responses API has OpenAI-specific response and tool-call shapes.
  - Gemini’s OpenAI-compatible endpoint expects the **chat-completions** flow, so you may need to adapt tool schemas and parsing accordingly.

In short: the notebook examples were written for the newer OpenAI Responses format, but Gemini works better through the older chat-completions interface, so the response handling and tool handling must be updated.

question: How do I get Pydantic structured output from Gemini if `responses.parse` isn’t supported through the OpenAI-compatible endpoint?
answer_llm:
Use the OpenAI SDK’s **chat completions parsing flow** instead of `responses.parse`.

1. Define a Pydantic model for the structure you want.
2. Call `client.chat.completions.parse(...)`
3. Read the parsed object from `response.choices[0].message.parsed`

Example:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from openai import OpenAI
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

class Question(BaseModel):
    question: str
    answer: str

class Questions(BaseModel):
    questions: list[Question]

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

messages = [
    {"role": "developer", "content": "Generate three FAQ questions about RAG."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Topic: structured output with Gemini"}
]

response = client.chat.completions.parse(
    model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite",
    messages=messages,
    response_format=Questions
)

result = response.choices[0].message.parsed
print(result.questions)
```

If you want, I can also show the non-parsing fallback using plain `chat.completions.create()` and manual JSON parsing.
answer_orig:
To get parsed structured output, use the OpenAI SDK's chat-completions parsing flow instead of the newer Responses API. This is the right choice when you want to stay on the OpenAI SDK but call a chat-completions-compatible model like Gemini through the OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

First, define the structure you want Gemini to return as a Pydantic model:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Question(BaseModel):
    question: str
    answer: str

class Questions(BaseModel):
    questions: list[Question]
```

Then call `chat.completions.parse` with `response_format=Questions`. Pass the same kind of `messages` you would to any chat completion:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

messages = [
    {"role": "developer", "content": "Generate three FAQ questions about RAG."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Topic: structured output with Gemini"}
]

response = client.chat.completions.parse(
    model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite",
    messages=messages,
    response_format=Questions
)

result = response.choices[0].message.parsed
print(result.questions)
```

Notes:
- This approach keeps using the OpenAI SDK while leveraging Gemini through Google's endpoint.
- The parsed output (e.g., `result.questions`) is a Pydantic object available directly, with no manual JSON parsing.
- `chat.completions.parse` requires `pydantic` (`uv add pydantic`).

question: How do I set up `response_format` and `messages` when calling Gemini via `client.chat.completions.parse`?
answer_llm:
Use the same **chat-completions-style messages** you would for any OpenAI-compatible call, and pass your Pydantic model class to `response_format`.

Example:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from openai import OpenAI
import os

class Question(BaseModel):
    question: str
    answer: str

class Questions(BaseModel):
    questions: list[Question]

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

messages = [
    {"role": "developer", "content": "Generate three FAQ questions about RAG."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Topic: structured output with Gemini"}
]

response = client.chat.completions.parse(
    model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite",
    messages=messages,
    response_format=Questions
)

result = response.choices[0].message.parsed
print(result.questions)
```

So:

- `messages` = normal chat messages like `developer` and `user`
- `response_format` = your Pydantic model, e.g. `Questions`

If you want, I can also show the non-`parse` version using plain JSON output.
answer_orig:
To get parsed structured output, use the OpenAI SDK's chat-completions parsing flow instead of the newer Responses API. This is the right choice when you want to stay on the OpenAI SDK but call a chat-completions-compatible model like Gemini through the OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

First, define the structure you want Gemini to return as a Pydantic model:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Question(BaseModel):
    question: str
    answer: str

class Questions(BaseModel):
    questions: list[Question]
```

Then call `chat.completions.parse` with `response_format=Questions`. Pass the same kind of `messages` you would to any chat completion:

```python
import os

from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

messages = [

    {"role": "user", "content": "Topic: structured output with Gemini"}
]

response = client.chat.completions.parse(
    model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite",
    messages=messages,
    response_format=Questions
)

result = response.choices[0].message.parsed
print(result.questions)
```

question: How do I install Ollama on Mac, Windows, or Linux?
answer_llm:
To install Ollama:

- **macOS**: Go to [https://ollama.com/download](https://ollama.com/download) and download the **`.pkg`** file, then install it.
- **Windows**: Go to [https://ollama.com/download](https://ollama.com/download) and download the **`.msi`** file, then install it.
- **Linux**: Run this in the terminal:

```bash
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
```

After installing, you can test it with:

```bash
ollama run llama3
```

question: Why does tiktoken say one number, but OpenAI’s usage shows a few more tokens for the same chat prompt?
answer_llm:
` tiktoken ` and OpenAI’s API can disagree because OpenAI adds a few **internal chat-format tokens** that `tiktoken` doesn’t include when you count only the visible text.

In particular:
- Each chat message adds about **4 structural tokens** for role/content/separators.
- OpenAI also adds **2 global tokens** to mark the assistant response start.
- Small things like extra whitespace, newlines, or unusual Unicode can add a bit more.

So your prompt might look like 320 tokens in `tiktoken`, but OpenAI may report 327 because of those extra internal tokens.
answer_orig:
When using `tiktoken.encode()` to count tokens in your prompt, you might see a difference compared to OpenAI’s API response. For instance, you might get 320 tokens, while OpenAI reports 327. This is due to internal tokens added by OpenAI’s chat formatting.

Here’s what happens:

- Each message in a `chat.completions.create()` call (e.g., `{role: "user", content: "..."}`) adds 4 structural tokens (role, content, separators).
- The API adds 2 tokens globally to mark the start of assistant response generation.
- Extra newlines, whitespace, or uncommon Unicode characters in your content may slightly increase the token count.

Thus, even if your visible text is 320 tokens, OpenAI may count 327 due to these internal additions.

question: Where do the extra tokens come from in a chat.completions request, even when my text looks shorter?
answer_llm:
The extra tokens come from OpenAI’s internal chat formatting, not just your visible text.

In a `chat.completions.create()` request:

- each message adds **4 structural tokens** for things like role/content separators
- the API adds **2 more tokens globally** to mark the start of assistant generation
- extra whitespace, newlines, or uncommon Unicode can add a few more tokens

So even if your prompt text looks shorter, OpenAI may count more tokens because of these hidden formatting tokens.

question: How can I keep my OpenAI API key loaded automatically instead of exporting it in every new terminal?
answer_llm:
Use **`dirdotenv`** to load it automatically when you enter the project directory.

Quick setup:

```bash
uv tool install dirdotenv

# add to ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc:
eval "$(dirdotenv hook bash)"   # or zsh
```

Then in your project:

```bash
echo 'OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...' > .env
echo '.env' >> .gitignore
```

After that, the key is loaded automatically whenever you `cd` into that folder.

If you already use it, **`direnv`** also works.
answer_orig:
Use [`dirdotenv`](https://github.com/alexeygrigorev/dirdotenv). It is like `direnv`, but works with both `.env` and `.envrc`, and is more portable across shells and operating systems.

```bash
uv tool install dirdotenv

# add this line to your ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc:
eval "$(dirdotenv hook bash)"   # or zsh

# inside your project:
echo 'OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...' > .env
echo '.env' >> .gitignore
```

After that, the key is loaded automatically when you `cd` into the project directory.

Important: always add `.env` and `.envrc` to `.gitignore` so keys never land on GitHub.

`direnv` is also fine if you already use it. In that case, create `.envrc`, add your exports there, and run `direnv allow`.

For GitHub Codespaces, use the built-in [Codespaces secrets](https://docs.github.com/en/codespaces/managing-your-codespaces/managing-your-account-specific-secrets-for-github-codespaces) instead of files in the repo.

For Python scripts, the equivalent is `python-dotenv`:

```python
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()  # loads .env from project root
```

Use [`dirdotenv`](https://github.com/alexeygrigorev/dirdotenv). It is like `direnv`, but works with both `.env` and `.envrc`, and is more portable across shells and operating systems.

```bash
uv tool install dirdotenv

# add this line to your ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc:
eval "$(dirdotenv hook bash)"   # or zsh

# inside your project:
echo 'OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...' > .env
echo '.env' >> .gitignore
```

After that, the key is loaded automatically when you `cd` into the project directory.

Important: always add `.env` and `.envrc` to `.gitignore` so keys never land on GitHub.

`direnv` is also fine if you already use it. In that case, create `.envrc`, add your exports there, and run `direnv allow`.

For GitHub Codespaces, use the built-in [Codespaces secrets](https://docs.github.com/en/codespaces/managing-your-codespaces/managing-your-account-specific-secrets-for-github-codespaces) instead of files in the repo.

For Python scripts, the equivalent is `python-dotenv`:

```python
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()  # loads .env from project root
```

question: How do I decide on field weights in minsearch without guessing too much?
answer_llm:
Use evaluation rather than guessing.

A systematic way is:

1. Create a small set of representative questions.
2. Mark which documents should be retrieved for each question.
3. Try different field weights in `minsearch`.
4. Compare the results using metrics like hit rate, precision@k, recall@k, or MRR.

For small projects, trial and error can work, but evaluating against a ground-truth set is the more reliable approach.

question: What’s the best way to clean documents before using them in RAG?
answer_llm:
The best approach is to make the documents **clean, structured, and easy to chunk and retrieve**.

In practice, that means:

- Remove obvious noise: broken HTML, duplicates, boilerplate, OCR errors, repeated headers and footers.
- Preserve useful context: titles, section names, dates, page numbers, speaker names, and Q&A structure.
- Store the text in a structured format that is easy to process, often JSON.
- Chunk the documents so related context stays together.
- Keep metadata that can help with filtering or ranking later.

The goal is not just to shorten the text, but to make retrieval more accurate.

- Chunk the documents in a way that keeps related context together.
- Keep metadata that may help filtering or ranking later.

The exact format depends on the source data. The goal is not just to make the text shorter, but to make retrieval more accurate.

question: In GitHub Codespaces, `uv` prints “Failed to hardlink files” — is that actually a problem or can I ignore it?
answer_llm:
No — it’s not actually a problem. In GitHub Codespaces, `uv` may not be able to hardlink files from its cache, so it warns and then falls back to copying them. The package still installs.

If you want to hide the warning, you can set:

```bash
export UV_LINK_MODE=copy
```

To make that permanent, add it to `~/.bashrc`:

```bash
echo 'export UV_LINK_MODE=copy' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```
answer_orig:
No. This warning can happen in GitHub Codespaces when `uv` cannot hardlink files between the cache and the target environment.

The package still installs. `uv` falls back to copying files.

To suppress the warning for the current shell:

```bash
export UV_LINK_MODE=copy
```

To make it persistent:

```bash
echo 'export UV_LINK_MODE=copy' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```

See the `uv` documentation for more details: [https://docs.astral.sh/uv/](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/).

question: If I keep coding in Codespaces but never commit, where does my work actually live?
answer_llm:
Your work lives inside the **Codespaces Linux VM** if you haven’t committed it yet.

question: Is my code inside Codespaces safe if the VM gets stopped or deleted?
answer_llm:

Uncommitted changes can survive a stop/start, but if you **delete or recreate** the codespace, they are lost. The best practice is to **commit and push your work regularly** so it’s stored in your GitHub repository.

question: Why does WSL2 say the model needs more memory even though my PC has enough RAM?
answer_llm:
WSL2 doesn’t use all of your PC’s RAM by default. It only sees the memory allocated to WSL2, which can be less than your total system RAM.

To give WSL2 more memory:

1. Create a `.wslconfig` file in `C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`
2. Add, for example:

```ini
[wsl2]
memory=8GB
```

3. Restart WSL:

```bash
wsl --shutdown
```

4. Check the available memory inside WSL with:

```bash
free
```

So the error means WSL2’s memory limit is too low, not that your whole PC lacks RAM.
answer_orig:
Your WSL2 is set to use Y.Y GiB, not all your computer memory. To allocate more RAM, follow these steps:

1. Create a `.wslconfig` file under your Windows user profile directory: `C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`.

2. Include the desired RAM allocation in the file:

   ```ini
   [wsl2]
   memory=8GB
   ```

3. Restart WSL using the command:

   ```bash
   wsl --shutdown
   ```

4. Run the `free` command in WSL to verify the changes.

For more details, read [this article](https://www.aleksandrhovhannisyan.com/blog/limiting-memory-usage-in-wsl-2/).

1. Create a `.wslconfig` file under your Windows user profile directory: `C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`.

2. Include the desired RAM allocation in the file:

   ```ini
   [wsl2]

   ```

3. Restart WSL using the command:

   ```bash
   wsl --shutdown
   ```

4. Run the `free` command in WSL to verify the changes.

question: My Codespace gets stuck on "Finishing up" and never opens — what’s the easiest thing to try first?
answer_llm:
The easiest first thing to try is:

1. Go to **github.com/codespaces**
2. **Stop** the codespace
3. **Start it again**

If it still gets stuck on “Finishing up,” try opening it in the **browser** instead of desktop VS Code, or use a different browser.
answer_orig:
These are usually GitHub Codespaces reliability issues rather than a problem with the course, so there's no single guaranteed fix — but the following workarounds resolve most cases.

**Codespace won't connect / stuck on "Finishing up":**

- Go to [github.com/codespaces](https://github.com/codespaces), stop the codespace, and start it again.
- If it still won't connect, open it in the **browser** instead of desktop VS Code, or try a different browser (Edge/Chrome/Brave).
- As a last resort, delete the codespace and create a new one.

**"My setup is all gone":**

- The repo in `/workspaces` persists across stop/start, but a **rebuild or a brand-new codespace** starts from a clean image, and system/global installs outside your project don't always survive. Reinstalling is quick with `uv` (`uv sync` / `uv add ...`).
- **Commit and push your work often** — uncommitted changes survive a stop/start but are lost if you delete or recreate the codespace.

If Codespaces keeps being flaky for you, consider running the course locally instead — see "[Can I run the course locally instead of Codespaces?](026_aa310de435_can-i-run-the-course-locally-instead-of-codespaces.md)".

question: Can I use a different LLM provider instead of OpenAI in the RAG module, or am I stuck with the course default?
answer_llm:
Yes — you can use a different LLM provider. The course is **not tied to OpenAI**.

You can switch to:
- **OpenAI-compatible hosted providers** like Groq, OpenRouter, DeepSeek, Gemini, Z.ai, Mistral
- **Local models** via Ollama, vLLM, LM Studio, etc.
- **Hugging Face hosted open models** like Qwen or Llama

Usually, if the provider is OpenAI-compatible, you only need to change:
- `base_url`
- API key
- model name

So you’re **not stuck with the course default**.
answer_orig:
If the provider used in the course isn't available or is blocked in your region (or you simply prefer another one), you can use any other LLM provider — the course isn't tied to OpenAI. Just switch to something else:

- Hosted, OpenAI-compatible providers — e.g. Groq, OpenRouter, DeepSeek, Gemini, Z.ai, Mistral. The course code uses the OpenAI client, so you usually only need to change the `base_url`, the API key, and the model name.
- Open models via Hugging Face (e.g. Qwen, Llama) if you prefer hosted open-source models.
- Serve a model locally with [Ollama](https://ollama.com/), [vLLM](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm), LM Studio, or anything else — no external API call at all, so regional blocks don't apply and you don't need a paid key. Most of these also expose an OpenAI-compatible endpoint, so the course code works with only a `base_url` change.
- Rent a GPU machine and serve the model there (e.g. with vLLM) if your own machine can't run the model you want. This gives you a private OpenAI-compatible endpoint to point the course code at — just remember to stop/delete the instance when you're done so you don't keep paying for it.
- A VPN also works if you just need to reach a provider that's blocked at the network level.

Anything with an OpenAI-compatible endpoint (or a locally served model) will work — pick whatever is available and convenient for you.

For a curated list of options, see [Awesome LLMs](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/awesome-llms.md) in the course repo, which collects OpenAI API alternatives and tools for running models locally.

question: How can I stop OpenAI 429 errors when I run notebooks that loop over lots of docs?
answer_llm:
Free-tier and low-spend OpenAI accounts have per-minute and per-day limits, so the best fix is to **reduce API calls before handling errors**:

- **Use retries with backoff**:
  ```python
  from openai import OpenAI
  client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
  ```
- **Lower concurrency**: keep thread/process pools small, around **2–3 workers**.
- **Cache results**: save embeddings/LLM outputs to disk and reuse them on reruns.
- **Batch requests** where possible instead of looping one doc at a time.
- **Use a cheaper/free model/provider**: `gpt-4o-mini` or Groq’s free tier (`llama-3.3-70b-versatile`).

If the error is actually **`insufficient_quota`**, that’s not a rate limit issue — it means your OpenAI account needs billing/credits added.
answer_orig:
Free-tier and low-spend OpenAI accounts have per-minute and per-day request limits that are easy to blow through when a notebook loops over many documents. Reduce the number of API calls before you worry about handling the error after the fact.

- **Retry with backoff.** The `openai` Python client retries automatically with exponential backoff; raise the limit so transient 429s resolve themselves instead of crashing the run:

  ```python
  from openai import OpenAI

  client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
  ```

- **Lower concurrency.** In a thread/process pool, keep the pool small (2-3 workers) so you stay under the per-minute cap. A larger pool finishes one batch fast and then fails on the next.
- **Cache results.** Write embeddings and LLM responses to disk (JSONL, pickle, or a vector DB) and reload on re-run. Re-executing a notebook should not re-call the API for inputs you already processed.
- **Batch where possible.** Group independent inputs into a single request (e.g. embed a list of texts in one call) instead of looping one-by-one.
- **Use a cheaper/free provider.** `gpt-4o-mini` is cheap enough for the whole course; Groq's free tier (`llama-3.3-70b-versatile`) has generous per-minute limits via the OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

If you already see `insufficient_quota`, that is a billing issue, not a rate limit — see [OpenAI: Error: RateLimitError: Error code: 429](openai-error-ratelimiterror-error-code-429).

Free-tier and low-spend OpenAI accounts have per-minute and per-day request limits that are easy to blow through when a notebook loops over many documents. Reduce the number of API calls before you worry about handling the error after the fact.

- **Retry with backoff.** The `openai` Python client retries automatically with exponential backoff; raise the limit so transient 429s resolve themselves instead of crashing the run:

  ```python
  from openai import OpenAI

  client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
  ```

- **Lower concurrency.** In a thread/process pool, keep the pool small (2-3 workers) so you stay under the per-minute cap. A larger pool finishes one batch fast and then fails on the next.
- **Cache results.** Write embeddings and LLM responses to disk (JSONL, pickle, or a vector DB) and reload on re-run. Re-executing a notebook should not re-call the API for inputs you already processed.
- **Batch where possible.** Group independent inputs into a single request (e.g. embed a list of texts in one call) instead of looping one-by-one.
- **Use a cheaper/free provider.** `gpt-4o-mini` is cheap enough for the whole course; Groq's free tier (`llama-3.3-70b-versatile`) has generous per-minute limits via the OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

If you already see `insufficient_quota`, that is a billing issue, not a rate limit — see [OpenAI: Error: RateLimitError: Error code: 429](openai-error-ratelimiterror-error-code-429).

question: In agentic RAG, what exactly counts as a tool or function the model can use?
answer_llm:
In agentic RAG, a tool/function is anything the model can call for external help. Examples include:

- searching documents
- querying a database
- calling an API
- running a calculation

The key point is that the model decides when to use it.

In the context of this course, tools and functions are closely related terms. Do not worry too much about the naming difference.

A tool/function is something the model can call when it needs external help, such as searching documents, querying a database, calling an API, or running a calculation.

The important idea is that in agentic RAG, the model can decide when to call a tool/function. In basic RAG, retrieval is usually a fixed step that happens before the model answers.

question: How is tool use in agentic RAG different from the usual retrieval step in basic RAG?
answer_llm:
In agentic RAG, tool use is **decided by the model during the interaction**: the model can choose when it needs help from a tool like search, a database, an API, or a calculation.

In basic RAG, the retrieval step is usually a **fixed preprocessing step** that happens **before** the model generates the answer.

So the main difference is:

- **Basic RAG:** retrieval is always done in a preset way first.
- **Agentic RAG:** the model can actively decide whether and when to call a tool.

question: Why do I get `AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'output'` when I call the new Responses API with a model that isn’t from OpenAI?
answer_llm:
Because the new OpenAI Responses API is OpenAI-specific. If you use `client.responses.create(...)` with a non-OpenAI model, the returned object may be just a string from that provider, not an OpenAI response object, so `response.output` doesn’t exist.

For non-OpenAI providers, use the chat-completions API instead:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="<provider-model>",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    tools=tools_schema,
)
return response.choices[0].message.content
```

You may also need to adapt the tools schema to the provider’s expected format.
answer_orig:
The new OpenAI Responses API (`client.responses.create(...)`, accessed via `response.output`) is OpenAI-specific. Other providers (Mistral, Groq, Gemini, etc.) don't implement it.

For non-OpenAI providers, use the chat-completions API and read `response.choices[0].message.content`:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="<provider-model>",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    tools=tools_schema,  # may need adapting per provider
)
return response.choices[0].message.content
```

You'll also have to adapt the tools schema to whatever shape your provider expects.

It is okay to use the older `chat.completions` API for homework or projects if your provider supports that interface better than the Responses API.

question: If I’m using Mistral, Groq, Gemini, or another provider, should I be using `response.output` or something else?
answer_llm:
Use `response.output` only with the **OpenAI Responses API**.

For **Mistral, Groq, Gemini, or other non-OpenAI providers**, you should usually use the **chat completions API** and read:

```python
response.choices[0].message.content
```

So:

- `response.output` → OpenAI Responses API only
- `response.choices[0].message.content` → chat-completions style responses from other providers

If the provider is OpenAI-compatible, you can often still use the OpenAI client, but the field you read depends on which API you called.

question: How do I fix the 400 error in Azure OpenAI that says tools[0].function is missing?
What should the get_weather_tool JSON look like so Azure OpenAI accepts it?
I keep getting an invalid_request_error about the function field in tools — what am I missing?

Why does my Azure OpenAI call fail with missing_required_parameter, and how should I define the weather tool?
Why do I get a 404 with toyaikit on Groq, and what should I switch to instead?
answer_llm:
With toyaikit, you get a 404 on Groq because the `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient` use OpenAI’s **Responses** endpoint (`responses.create`), and that endpoint is OpenAI-only.

You should switch to the **chat completions** classes instead:

- `OpenAIChatCompletionsClient`
- `OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner`

For Groq, point an OpenAI client at Groq’s base URL:

```python
from openai import OpenAI
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIChatCompletionsClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner

groq_client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://api.groq.com/openai/v1",
)

llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(
    model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile",
    client=groq_client,
)
```

If you want Anthropic instead, use:

- `AnthropicClient`
- `AnthropicMessagesRunner`
answer_orig:
If you get a `404` (or similar) when using toyaikit with Groq or another non-OpenAI provider, it's because `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient` call OpenAI's Responses endpoint (`responses.create`), which only OpenAI implements. Switch to the **chat completions** classes, which use the standard `chat.completions.create` endpoint that Groq and other OpenAI-compatible providers support.

### OpenAI or Groq (chat completions)

```python
import os
from openai import OpenAI
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIChatCompletionsClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)   # functions need type hints + an Args: docstring

# OpenAI:
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="gpt-4o-mini", client=OpenAI())

# Groq (same runner, just point the OpenAI client at Groq's base URL):
groq_client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://api.groq.com/openai/v1",
)
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile", client=groq_client)

runner = OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

### Anthropic

```python
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import AnthropicClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import AnthropicMessagesRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)

# Reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from the environment automatically:
llm_client = AnthropicClient(model="claude-haiku-4-5")

runner = AnthropicMessagesRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

Notes:

- `runner.loop(prompt=...)` runs one turn programmatically and returns a result with `.last_message`, `.all_messages`, and `.tokens` — no chat interface needed. Use `runner.run()` only for the interactive Jupyter loop.
- Pass an explicit, current model id. For Anthropic, `claude-haiku-4-5` / `claude-sonnet-4-5` work; an outdated id can 404.
- For Groq you'll see a harmless `UnknownModelWarning: No pricing data...` — the call still succeeds, only cost calculation is skipped.

question: Does toyaikit need the OpenAI Responses API, or can it work with chat completions on other providers?
answer_llm:
No. Toyaikit does **not** need the OpenAI Responses API specifically.

For **non-OpenAI providers**, you should use the **chat completions** API instead. The context says the Responses API (`responses.create` / `response.output`) is OpenAI-specific, while providers like Groq, Mistral, Gemini, etc. can work with `chat.completions.create` and `response.choices[0].message.content`.

So:
- **OpenAI Responses API**: only for OpenAI
- **Chat completions**: works with other providers, if they support that interface

If needed, toyaikit also has chat-completions runners for OpenAI-compatible providers and Anthropic-specific support.

If you get a `404` (or similar) when using toyaikit with Groq or another non-OpenAI provider, it's because `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient` call OpenAI's Responses endpoint (`responses.create`), which only OpenAI implements. Switch to the **chat completions** classes, which use the standard `chat.completions.create` endpoint that Groq and other OpenAI-compatible providers support.

### OpenAI or Groq (chat completions)

```python

from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIChatCompletionsClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)   # functions need type hints + an Args: docstring

# OpenAI:
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="gpt-4o-mini", client=OpenAI())

# Groq (same runner, just point the OpenAI client at Groq's base URL):
groq_client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://api.groq.com/openai/v1",
)
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile", client=groq_client)

runner = OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

question: Why do I get a 402 error from OpenRouter when I call responses.create() in homework code, and what should I change so it works?
answer_llm:
You get the 402 because OpenRouter checks the **maximum possible output tokens** when `responses.create()` is called, and if you don’t set a reasonable `max_output_tokens`, it may assume a very large value and reject the request before the model runs.

What to change:
- Add a lower `max_output_tokens` value, such as `1024`.

Example:

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

If you’re using the Module 1 homework `rag_helper.py`, add the same parameter in the `llm()` method.

If you’re using ToyAIKit in Q6:

```python
llm_client = OpenAIClient(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    extra_kwargs={"max_output_tokens": 1024},
)
```

Also make sure your `.env` is pointing to OpenRouter correctly:

```env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-...
OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
OPENAI_MODEL=openai/gpt-oss-120b:free
```

If it still fails, check your OpenRouter key limits/credits.
answer_orig:
OpenRouter can return APIStatusError with code 402 when responses.create() is called without a reasonable max_output_tokens limit. This happens because OpenRouter bills/limits checks against the maximum possible output (which can be very large, around 65536 tokens), so a free or low-limit key can be rejected before the model runs. This is different from a direct OpenAI endpoint (which typically returns 429 for insufficient quota).

Fix

Pass a lower limit in your responses.create() call:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI()  # uses OPENAI_API_KEY and OPENAI_BASE_URL from .env

response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework with rag_helper.py, add the same parameter in the ``llm()`` method.

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework Q6 with ToyAIKit:

```python
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIClient

llm_client = OpenAIClient(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    extra_kwargs={"max_output_tokens": 1024},
)
```

1024 is enough for homework answers; you can raise it later if needed.

If it still fails

1) Confirm your `.env` points at OpenRouter:

```env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-...
OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
OPENAI_MODEL=openai/gpt-oss-120b:free
```

2) Check your OpenRouter key limit and remaining credits at https://openrouter.ai/settings/keys

3) Prefer a pinned model (for example `openai/gpt-oss-120b:free`) instead of `openrouter/free`, which can route to models with different limits.

Note: This behavior is different from OpenAI’s typical 429 handling for insufficient quota. If you still encounter issues after these steps, double-check the model and endpoint configuration to ensure the key is valid and has sufficient credits.

question: How should I count tokens for the Module 1 homework if I'm using a model other than OpenAI?
answer_llm:
For Module 1 homework, you should get the token count from the **model’s response object**, not from `tiktoken`.

- If the provider is OpenAI-compatible, look for fields like:
  - `response.usage.input_tokens`
  - `response.usage.prompt_tokens`

- If you use a non-OpenAI provider, check that provider’s own usage/token fields and adapt your code.

- Do **not** use `tiktoken` or `cl100k_base` as a generic tokenizer for Gemini, Mistral, Hugging Face, Groq, or other providers, because tokenization differs.

- If the provider does not expose token usage, use the provider’s native tokenizer as an approximation.

If it’s a multiple-choice question, choose the closest option.

question: Why does the homework checker say my repo link is not returning 200, and what should I change?
answer_llm:
The checker is doing a GET request to your repo URL and expects an HTTP `200`. If it gets `404`, `500`, or anything else, it usually means the repo isn’t publicly reachable at the link you submitted.

What to change:
- Make the repo **public**
- Submit the **plain repo URL**, not one ending in `.git`
  - Good: `https://github.com/you/repo`
  - Bad: `https://github.com/you/repo.git`
- Double-check for a **typo** in the URL

Quick test: open the link in a private/incognito window. If it loads there, the checker should be able to access it too.
answer_orig:
The submission checker fetches the URL you submit with a GET request and expects an HTTP `200` response. A non-200 status (404, 500, etc.) almost always means the link isn't publicly reachable as-is. Check the usual causes:

- **Private repo** — make the repository public so the checker can access it.
- **Trailing `.git`** — submit the plain repository URL (e.g. `https://github.com/you/repo`), not `https://github.com/you/repo.git`.
- **Typo in the URL** — paste the exact link.

A quick test: open the URL in a private/incognito browser window. If it loads for you there, it'll work for the checker too.

question: I submitted my GitHub URL but it gets a 404 or 500 — could the repo privacy or the URL format be the problem?
answer_llm:

- **Private repo** → make it public.
- **Wrong URL format** → submit the plain repo URL like `https://github.com/you/repo`, **not** `https://github.com/you/repo.git`.
- **Typo in the link** → copy/paste the exact URL.

A quick test is to open the link in an **incognito/private window**. If it loads there, it should work for the checker too.

question: What exactly are embeddings in vector search, and why do they matter?
answer_llm:
Embeddings are numerical vector representations of text, images, or other non-numerical data. They convert that data into numbers in a way that preserves meaning and context: similar inputs should produce similar vectors.

Why they matter in vector search:
- They let us measure semantic similarity mathematically.
- We can compare vectors using dot product or cosine similarity.
- This makes it possible to retrieve items with similar meaning, not just matching keywords.

In short: embeddings turn meaning into numbers, which is what makes vector search work.
answer_orig: Embeddings refer to the process of converting non-numerical data into numerical data while preserving meaning and context. When similar non-numerical data is input into an embedding algorithm, it should yield similar numerical data. The proximity of these numerical values allows for the use of mathematical semantic similarity algorithms. Related concepts include the "vector space model" and "dimensionality reduction."

question: How does turning text into numbers keep the meaning and context?
answer_llm: Turning text into numbers keeps the meaning and context by mapping similar pieces of text to similar numerical vectors. So texts with related meaning end up close together in vector space, which preserves semantic similarity and context.
answer_orig: Embeddings refer to the process of converting non-numerical data into numerical data while preserving meaning and context. When similar non-numerical data is input into an embedding algorithm, it should yield similar numerical data. The proximity of these numerical values allows for the use of mathematical semantic similarity algorithms. Related concepts include the "vector space model" and "dimensionality reduction."

question: Why do similar pieces of non-numerical data end up with close numerical values after embedding?
answer_llm: Because the embedding algorithm is designed to preserve meaning and context: if two pieces of non-numerical data are similar, it maps them to nearby points in the vector space, so their numerical values end up close together.
answer_orig: Embeddings refer to the process of converting non-numerical data into numerical data while preserving meaning and context. When similar non-numerical data is input into an embedding algorithm, it should yield similar numerical data. The proximity of these numerical values allows for the use of mathematical semantic similarity algorithms. Related concepts include the "vector space model" and "dimensionality reduction."

question: How can those numbers be used to measure semantic similarity between texts?
answer_llm: Those numbers can be used as vectors representing texts. By comparing the vectors with cosine similarity, you measure how closely the texts point in the same direction: values near 1 mean very similar content, near 0 mean little to no similarity, and -1 mean opposite vectors. If the vectors are normalized, the dot product between them is enough to compute this similarity efficiently.
answer_orig: Embeddings refer to the process of converting non-numerical data into numerical data while preserving meaning and context. When similar non-numerical data is input into an embedding algorithm, it should yield similar numerical data. The proximity of these numerical values allows for the use of mathematical semantic similarity algorithms. Related concepts include the "vector space model" and "dimensionality reduction."

question: Is embedding the same thing as the vector space model or dimensionality reduction, or are they different concepts?
answer_llm:
They’re related, but not exactly the same.

- **Embeddings** are the general idea/process of converting non-numerical data into numerical vectors while preserving meaning and context.
- The **vector space model** is a related concept/framework where data is represented in a vector space so similarity can be measured mathematically.
- **Dimensionality reduction** is also related, but it’s a broader technique for reducing the number of dimensions in data.

So: **embedding is not identical to either one**, but it is closely connected to both.

When similar non-numerical data is input into an embedding algorithm, it should yield similar numerical data. The proximity of these numerical values allows for the use of mathematical semantic similarity algorithms. Related concepts include the "vector space model" and "dimensionality reduction."

question: Why did the lesson just use dot product for similarity instead of explicitly doing cosine similarity?
answer_llm:
Because the lesson assumed the embeddings were already normalized to unit length. In that case, the dot product is mathematically the same as cosine similarity, so there was no need to write cosine similarity explicitly.

In the homework, the vectors are not necessarily normalized by default, so you need to normalize them first if you want dot product to equal cosine similarity.
answer_orig:
In the lesson, `.dot(...)` was used under the assumption that the embeddings returned by the model (e.g., `model.encode(...)` from OpenAI) are already normalized to have unit length. In that case, the dot product is mathematically equivalent to cosine similarity.

In the homework, however, we use classic embeddings like TF-IDF + SVD, which are not normalized by default. This means that the dot product does not represent cosine similarity unless we manually normalize the vectors.

question: In the homework, why do we need to normalize the vectors before comparing them?
answer_llm:
We need to normalize the vectors so that the dot product becomes true cosine similarity.

In the homework, the embeddings are not normalized by default, so if you skip normalization, `.dot(...)` will include vector length as well as direction. After normalization, each vector has unit length, and then:

- `dot(a, b) = cosine_similarity(a, b)`

So normalization is needed to make the comparison meaningful and consistent with cosine similarity.

question: When doing vector search, do I need to embed just the answer, just the question, or both together?
answer_llm:
There's no single right answer — it's something to experiment with on your dataset.

The course suggests trying three options:
- **Embed the answer/text only**
- **Embed the question only**
- **Embed `question + " " + text`**

In practice, **`question + text` is often the best**, but it’s a bit longer and slightly more expensive. The right choice is the one that gives the best **hit rate / MRR** on your ground-truth set.
answer_orig:
There's no single right answer — it's an experiment to run on your dataset. The course shows three options:

- Embed the answer (`text`) only — works because the model captures semantic similarity between questions and their answers.
- Embed the question only — works because user queries look like the indexed questions.
- Embed `question + " " + text` — often the best, but produces longer input and slightly more cost.

Pick whichever gives the best hit rate / MRR on your ground-truth set. The course materials include a side-by-side comparison.

question: What does cosine similarity actually measure between two vectors?
answer_llm:
Cosine similarity measures the **angle/alignment** between two vectors, not their raw size.

- **1** means the vectors point in the same direction
- **0** means they are orthogonal, or unrelated in direction
- **-1** means they point in opposite directions

So in practice, it tells you **how similar the directions of two vectors are**.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity is a measure used to calculate the similarity between two non-zero vectors, often used in text analysis to determine how similar two documents are based on their content. This metric computes the cosine of the angle between two vectors, which are typically word counts or TF-IDF values of the documents. The cosine similarity value ranges from -1 to 1, where 1 indicates that the vectors are identical, 0 indicates that the vectors are orthogonal (no similarity), and -1 represents completely opposite vectors.

question: Why is cosine similarity commonly used for comparing documents in text analysis?
answer_llm: Cosine similarity is commonly used because it measures how closely two documents point in the same direction in vector space, based on their content, rather than their length. This makes it useful for comparing documents represented as word counts, TF-IDF, or embeddings, especially when you want similarity to depend on topic overlap instead of document size.

This metric computes the cosine of the angle between two vectors, which are typically word counts or TF-IDF values of the documents. The cosine similarity value ranges from -1 to 1, where 1 indicates that the vectors are identical, 0 indicates that the vectors are orthogonal (no similarity), and -1 represents completely opposite vectors.

question: How do you calculate cosine similarity for vectors like word counts or TF-IDF?
answer_llm:
For word-count or TF-IDF vectors, cosine similarity is computed as the cosine of the angle between the two vectors.

If the vectors are already normalized to unit length, you can calculate it with a simple dot product:

\[
\text{cosine similarity} = \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}
\]

If they are not normalized, use the full formula:

\[
\text{cosine similarity} = \frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{\|\vec{a}\| \, \|\vec{b}\|}
\]

So in practice:

1. Compute the dot product of the two vectors.
2. Compute the length (norm) of each vector.
3. Divide the dot product by the product of the norms.

The result ranges from -1 to 1:
- 1 = identical direction
- 0 = orthogonal, no similarity
- -1 = opposite direction

question: Why does cosine similarity turn into a dot product once the vectors are normalized?
answer_llm:
Because for two vectors \(a\) and \(b\),

\[
\cos(\theta) = \frac{a \cdot b}{\|a\|\|b\|}
\]

If both vectors are normalized to unit length, then \(\|a\| = \|b\| = 1\), so:

\[
\cos(\theta) = a \cdot b
\]

So once vectors are normalized, cosine similarity is just the dot product.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity measures how aligned two vectors are, regardless of their magnitude. When all vectors (including the query) are normalized to unit length, their magnitudes no longer matter. In this case, cosine similarity is equivalent to simply taking the dot product between the query and each document embedding. This allows us to compute similarities efficiently using matrix multiplication.

question: If all embeddings have unit length, why can we just use matrix multiplication to get cosine scores?
answer_llm:
Because if all embeddings are normalized to unit length, cosine similarity is just the dot product.

Cosine similarity measures the angle between two vectors, and when both vectors have length 1, the magnitude part drops out. So for a query vector and a matrix of unit-length embeddings, you can compute all cosine scores with a single matrix multiplication.

question: How does normalization make similarity search faster with embeddings?
answer_llm:
Normalization makes similarity search faster because it lets cosine similarity be computed as a simple dot product.

When embeddings are normalized to unit length, their magnitudes don’t matter anymore, so:

- cosine similarity = dot product
- all document vectors can be compared to a query using one matrix multiplication

That is much faster than computing angles or norms for each pair individually.

question: Why is cosine similarity basically the same as multiplying the embedding matrix by the query vector?
answer_llm:
Because if the embeddings and the query are **normalized to unit length**, cosine similarity is just the **dot product**.

So instead of computing the cosine formula for each vector separately, you can:

- stack all document embeddings into a matrix
- multiply that matrix by the query vector

That matrix multiplication gives you the cosine similarity scores for all documents at once, efficiently.

question: Why do my dot-product scores from NumPy and a Python loop not match exactly, even though the code should be the same?
answer_llm:
Because of floating-point precision.

Even if the NumPy `dot` and your Python loop are mathematically the same, they may add the numbers in a different order:

- `X.dot(v_query)` uses optimized BLAS code
- a Python loop sums sequentially

Floating-point addition is not perfectly associative, so the last few decimal places can differ. The scores are usually still correct.

Use a tolerance check instead of exact equality, for example:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```

If you want, I can also show a tiny example where two “same” dot products differ by a small amount.
answer_orig:
In [Lesson 4 (Vector Search)](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/02-vector-search/lessons/04-vector-search.md) we compute the scores two ways — `scores = X.dot(v_query)` and the equivalent for-loop — and they can come out slightly different. This is normal, it's just floating-point precision. If you compare them with `np.allclose(scores, scores_loop)` you might get `False`, even though both compute the same dot products. They just add the numbers up in a slightly different order, and floating-point addition isn't perfectly associative, so the results can differ in the last few decimal places.

question: Why do I get "IndexError: list index out of range" when I try to access the best chunk after embedding my document chunks?
answer_llm:
The error usually means the number of embeddings doesn’t match the number of chunks.

Make sure you generate embeddings directly from the chunk list, like this:

```python
contents = [chunk["content"] for chunk in chunks]
X = embedder.encode_batch(contents)
```

Then the number of rows in `X` should equal `len(chunks)`. If they don’t match, trying to access the “best chunk” can lead to `IndexError: list index out of range`.

```python

```

The number of rows in `X` should be equal to `len(chunks)`.

question: How do I fix the mismatch between embeddings and chunks so my vector search doesn’t break with an index error?
answer_llm:
Make sure the embeddings are created from the **same chunk list** you use for retrieval, so the number of embedding rows matches the number of chunks.

Use the chunks directly:

```python
contents = [chunk["content"] for chunk in chunks]
X = embedder.encode_batch(contents)
```

Then ensure:

```python
len(chunks) == X.shape[0]
```

If you build embeddings from `documents` but search over `chunks`, you can get an `IndexError` because the indices no longer align.

question: For Module 2 homework Q2, my cosine similarity doesn’t match any answer choice — could I be using the wrong embedding model?
answer_llm:
Yes — the most common reason is using a different embedding model than the homework expects.

For Module 2 Homework Q2, you should use the lightweight ONNX `Embedder` from `embedder.py`, not `sentence-transformers`:

```python
from embedder import Embedder
model = Embedder()
```

If you use a different model such as `multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1` or `all-mpnet-base-v2`, your cosine similarity may not match any answer choice.

Also double-check that you are:

- embedding the page’s `content` field
- comparing it to the query vector from Q1
- using normalized vectors

Since the `Embedder` returns normalized vectors, the dot product is the cosine similarity directly.

If your result is still slightly off after using the correct model, choose the closest option.
answer_orig:
The most common cause is using a different embedding model than the homework specifies. Homework 2 tells you **not** to use `sentence-transformers` and to use the lightweight ONNX `Embedder` from `embedder.py` instead:

```python
from embedder import Embedder
model = Embedder()
```

Both approaches produce the same vectors for the same model, but if you embed with a different model (for example the `multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1` model from the lessons, or `all-mpnet-base-v2`) you'll get a cosine value that isn't among the options.

Also check that:

- You're embedding the page's `content` field (not the filename or the whole dict).
- You're comparing against the query vector from Q1.
- The vectors are normalized — the `Embedder` returns normalized vectors, so the dot product is the cosine similarity directly.

If your value is still slightly off after using the right model, pick the closest option — small numerical differences are expected.

question: How do I set up Gemini, OpenAI, and Tavily API keys for the Kestra exercises?
answer_llm:
Set them in your terminal **before starting Kestra**.

For the Kestra exercises, use:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"                              # used by AI Copilot
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n $GEMINI_API_KEY | base64)             # used by the flows
export SECRET_OPENAI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-openai-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for flow 3
export SECRET_TAVILY_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-tavily-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for web search (flows 3, 5, 6)
```

Then start or restart Kestra:

```bash
docker compose up -d
```

Inside flows, reference the secret **without** the `SECRET_` prefix:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```

Important: the Kestra AI Copilot uses the plain `GEMINI_API_KEY`, while the flows use the base64-encoded `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY`.

question: Why does Kestra need both GEMINI_API_KEY and SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY, and which one does each part use?
answer_llm:
Kestra needs both because they are used by different parts:

- `GEMINI_API_KEY` = the **plain** API key
  - Used by the **AI Copilot**
- `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` = the **base64-encoded** version
  - Used by the **flows** inside Kestra

So:

- **Copilot** reads `GEMINI_API_KEY`
- **Flows** read `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY`

Inside a flow, you reference it as:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```

So the `SECRET_` prefix is for Kestra’s secret storage mechanism, while the Copilot needs the unencoded plain key.

question: Why does Kestra Copilot keep saying it can only help with creating flows, and what setting am I missing?
answer_llm:
Kestra Copilot says that when it **didn’t get a valid Gemini API key**, so it falls back to a canned refusal.

What you’re probably missing is the **plain** environment variable:

- **Copilot needs:** `GEMINI_API_KEY`
- **Flows may use:** `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY`

So set the plain key before starting Kestra, then restart it:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
docker compose up -d
```

Also check:

question: Why does my Kestra flow say the Gemini key is missing even though I already set SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY?
answer_llm:
The most likely issue is that `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` was encoded incorrectly.

Common causes:
- You used `echo` instead of `echo -n`, which adds a trailing newline.
- There was a trailing space in the value before encoding.
- Kestra was not restarted after setting the variable.
- In the flow, you referenced the secret incorrectly.

What to do:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
docker compose up -d
```

Also make sure:
- You restart Kestra after exporting the variable.
- You reference it in the flow as:
  ```yaml
  {{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
  ```
  not `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY`.
- You set the variable in the same shell session used to start Kestra.

If you want, I can also help you check whether your current base64 value is malformed.
answer_orig:
The most common cause is a corrupted base64 value. If you encode the key with a trailing space or newline, the decoded secret is wrong and authentication fails.

Use `echo -n` (which omits the trailing newline) and quote the value:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Other things to check:

- You must **restart** Kestra after exporting, so the container picks up the new variables: `docker compose up -d` (or `docker compose down && docker compose up -d`).
- Reference the secret in flows **without** the `SECRET_` prefix: `{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}`.
- Export the variables in the same shell session you start Kestra from.

A cleaner alternative is to put the keys in a `.env` file and pass it explicitly:

```bash
docker compose --env-file ./.env -f ./docker-compose.yml up -d
```

question: Could a bad base64 encode be breaking my API key in Kestra, and how do I avoid that?
answer_llm:
Yes — a bad base64 encode can absolutely break the secret Kestra reads.

To avoid it, encode the key like this:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Key points:
- use `echo -n` so you don’t add a trailing newline
- quote the variable: `"$GEMINI_API_KEY"`
- restart Kestra after exporting the variable
- in your flow, reference it as:
  ```yaml
  {{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
  ```

If you want, I can also show you a quick way to verify whether the base64 value was encoded correctly.

question: Why can't we just use a notebook for this instead of an orchestrator like Kestra?
answer_llm:
Notebooks are great for learning and experimenting, but they’re not enough for real AI workflows.

An orchestrator like Kestra gives you things a notebook doesn’t handle well, such as:

- scheduling jobs
- retries if something fails
- monitoring and visibility
- secret management
- reliably chaining multiple tasks together

So the reason we use Kestra is not because notebooks can’t run code, but because production-style workflows need more than a one-off script in cells. In Module 3, Kestra is also the tool the course uses to teach AI Copilot, RAG, and AI agents in a workflow setting.

If you want, I can also explain this in a “notebook vs orchestrator” comparison table.
answer_orig:
Notebooks are great for learning and experimenting, but real AI workflows need more than a script that runs once: scheduling, retries, monitoring, secret management, and reliably chaining tasks together. That's what an orchestrator like Kestra provides.

In this module Kestra is also the vehicle for the AI techniques the course is teaching: AI Copilot to generate flows from natural language, RAG to ground responses in real data, and AI agents that decide which tools to call. The goal is to see how AI fits into production-style workflows, not just notebook cells.

Kestra's AI plugins also work with any major provider (OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic, and more), so you can swap providers in a flow without changing anything else. See the [module intro](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/01-intro.md) for the full motivation.

question: What does Kestra actually add that a plain script or notebook doesn't have?
answer_llm:
Kestra adds the things a plain script or notebook typically lacks in real workflows:

- **Scheduling**: run jobs automatically on a timetable
- **Retries**: recover from temporary failures
- **Monitoring**: see what ran and whether it succeeded
- **Secret management**: handle credentials safely
- **Chaining tasks reliably**: connect multiple steps into one workflow

A notebook is great for learning and experimenting, but Kestra is useful when you need a more production-style workflow.

question: Is there a way to build a Kestra flow without writing YAML by hand?
answer_llm:
Yes. You don’t have to write YAML by hand.

Kestra offers:
- a **no-code form editor** to build flows from a form,
- an **AI Copilot** that generates a flow from natural language,
- and **external agent skills** like Claude Code, Codex, or Kestra CTL to generate and push flows from your editor.

If you want, I can also explain which of these is best for your use case.
answer_orig:
Yes. You have three options that require no hand-written YAML or code:

- **The no-code form editor** builds a flow from a form instead of YAML. It is form-based (not drag-and-drop like n8n), so it suits people who don't want to edit YAML directly.
- **The AI Copilot** generates a flow from a natural-language description, and you can iterate with follow-up messages to add tasks or change the order. See the [AI Copilot lesson](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/04-ai-copilot.md).
- **External agent skills** (Claude Code, Codex, Kestra CTL) let you generate flows and push them into Kestra from your editor without touching the Kestra UI.

Unlike Airflow, where business logic and orchestration logic are intertwined in Python, Kestra keeps the two separate: your Python script stays a plain script, and the workflow YAML just describes how and when to run it. This is closer to a cron job than to a traditional Airflow DAG.

For simple point-to-point automations (e.g. "post a Slack message when a YouTube video goes live"), a tool like Zapier or n8n may be simpler and cheaper. Kestra's advantage is observability, retries, and handling more complex or technical workflows in one place.

question: Why does ChatGPT sometimes invent Kestra plugin fields like bucket or name instead of the real ones?
answer_llm:
Because ChatGPT is not grounded in the exact Kestra plugin schema for your running version. It can guess “plausible” field names, but those guesses may be invalid, so it invents things like `bucket` or `name` instead of the real property.

Examples from the course:

- **GCS Upload**: it may invent `bucket`/`name`, but the real field is `to`
- **BigQuery LoadFromGcs**: it may invent `projectId`/`dataset`/`table`, but the real field is `destinationTable`

How to avoid it:

- Cross-check against the official Kestra plugin docs
- Use Kestra’s built-in AI Copilot, which is grounded in the current schema
- Validate the YAML in your Kestra environment

Also, plugin properties can change by version, so what looks right in one release may be wrong in another.
answer_orig:
A general AI (e.g., ChatGPT) isn't grounded in Kestra's plugin schemas for your running version, so it can surface plausible but invalid property names (such as `bucket`/`name`) instead of the real, version-specific property. For example:

**GCS Upload** — invented `bucket`/`name` props vs. the real `to` property:

```yaml
# Incorrect (invented props)
- id: upload_to_gcs
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.gcs.Upload"
  bucket: "my-bucket"
  name: "path/to/file"

# Correct
- id: upload_to_gcs
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.gcs.Upload"
  to: "gs://my-bucket/path/to/file"
```

**BigQuery LoadFromGcs** — split `projectId`/`dataset`/`table` vs. the real `destinationTable`:

```yaml
# Incorrect (pseudo-split properties)
- id: load_to_bq
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.bigquery.LoadFromGcs"
  projectId: "my-project"
  dataset: "my_dataset"
  table: "my_table"

# Correct
- id: load_to_bq
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.bigquery.LoadFromGcs"
  destinationTable: "my-project.my_dataset.my_table"
```

To avoid this:

- Cross-check generated YAML against the official plugin docs: https://kestra.io/plugins/plugin-gcp
- Use Kestra's built-in AI Copilot, which is grounded in the current plugin schema for your running version.
- Validate and test your YAML in your Kestra environment to ensure it parses and runs as expected.

Note that plugin properties can vary by plugin version; what's correct in one release may be invalid in another. If you share a snippet, we can help verify it against the docs.
